summary: American Postmodern architect (1925–2018), known for the quip 'Less is a bore.' Authored Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture and co-authored Learning from Las Vegas with his wife Denise Scott Brown and Steven Izenour. Key buildings include the Vanna House, the Sainsbury Wing of London's National Gallery, and the Seattle Art Museum. Won the 1991 Pritzker Prize.

works.name: Vanna House (Vanna Venturi House)
works.description: House built for Venturi's mother Vanna in Philadelphia. Features an exposed chimney, a wrap-around staircase, a gently sloping roof, and a large monumental facade with a split gable. Described by critic Frederick Schwartz as 'the first Postmodern anything.' Located near the Esherick House by Louis Kahn. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Designed for his mother Vanna; exposed chimney, wrap-around staircase, sloping roof, monumental facade with a split gable
examples: A staircase surrounds the central chimney above a large facade created by a sloping roof in a home built for this man's mother, Vanna.
This architect created a house with an exposed chimney, a wrap-around staircase, and a gently sloping roof for his mother.
One structure designed by this architect was described by Frederick Schwartz as the 'first Postmodern anything' for its monumental facade and was designed for his mother Vanna in Philadelphia.

works.name: Learning from Las Vegas
works.description: 1972 book co-authored by Venturi, his wife Denise Scott Brown, and Steven Izenour. Celebrated roadside decor and the Las Vegas strip. Divided buildings into two types: 'ducks' (where the shape represents the building's function) and 'decorated sheds.' Aron Vinegar's I am a Monument is a critical examination of this book. Wikipedia

clues.clue: 1972 manifesto co-authored with Denise Scott Brown and Steven Izenour; celebrated roadside decor and the Las Vegas strip; divided buildings into 'ducks' and 'decorated sheds'
examples: Steven Izenour, Denise Scott Brown, and this creator of the Vanna House wrote the book Learning from Las Vegas.
a book co-written by this man, his wife Denise Scott Brown, and Steven Izenour that celebrated roadside decor and the strip of the title city.
Buildings whose shape represents their purpose are termed 'ducks' and contrasted with 'decorated' architecture in a book titled for this city.

works.name: Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture
works.description: 1966 book by Venturi that attacked modernism with a call for 'messy vitality' and an eclectic style. Defined his Postmodern philosophy in contrast to Mies van der Rohe's minimalism. Recipient of this award has been given to Venturi; the Pritzker Prize citation notes this book. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Called for an eclectic style of 'messy vitality'; argued for 'ugly and ordinary architecture'; attacked modernism
examples: He called for an eclectic style of 'messy vitality' in his book Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture
This architect of the Lieb House argued for 'ugly and ordinary architecture' in a work that divided buildings into 'ducks' and 'decorated sheds.'

works.name: Fire Station No. 4, Columbus, Indiana
works.description: Fire station designed by Venturi in Columbus, Indiana, featuring a giant yellow (gold) '4' at the top, with a hose-drying tower inscribed with the number placed in the middle separating equipment and living quarters, so the whole structure looks like an overturned number four. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Fire station with a giant gold No. 4 at the top; hose-drying tower in the middle; whole structure resembles an overturned number four
examples: A giant yellow four appears at the top of a fire station designed by this architect in Columbus, Indiana.

This architect designed a building with a hose-drying tower, inscribed with a gold No. 4, in the middle separating the equipment and living quarters, so the whole structure looks like an overturned number four.

works.name: Sainsbury Wing (National Gallery, London)
works.description: Addition to the National Gallery in London, designed with Denise Scott Brown. Denise Scott Brown refused to attend the 1991 Pritzker Prize ceremony because Venturi was awarded sole credit for the work on which they had collaborated. Also designed the Seattle Art Museum. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Addition to London's National Gallery; Denise Scott Brown refused to attend the 1991 Pritzker ceremony because Venturi received sole credit
examples: This architect's partner on the Sainsbury Wing of London's National Gallery refused to attend his 1991 Pritzker Prize ceremony since he was given sole awardship on projects like the Seattle Art Museum.
He created a house that exposes its chimney...and bunched pilasters to create a concertina-like effect on the facade of a museum addition co-designed with his wife Denise Scott Brown.

works.name: Other Works / General
works.description: Other works include: the Lieb House (with a semicircle 'sailboat' window and a huge number nine beside the door; had to be floated up the East River to escape New Jersey); Franklin Court in Philadelphia (with two steel-tube 'ghost structures' in the shape of homes); the Allen Art Museum addition at Oberlin; the Brant-Johnson Ski House in Vail (built almost entirely of pine planks); the Haute-Garonne Capitol Building in Toulouse; and the never-built Bill-Ding-Building for the NFL Hall of Fame in New Brunswick. His quip 'Less is a bore' plays on Mies van der Rohe's 'Less is more.'

clues.clue: 'Less is a bore' — quip targeting Mies van der Rohe's 'Less is more'; associated with Postmodernism
examples: identify this American architect linked with postmodernism through his quip 'less is a bore.'
Venturi's quip that 'less is a bore' plays on this other architect's maxim that 'less is more.'

clues.clue: Lieb House: semicircle 'sailboat' window, number nine beside the front door; had to be floated up the East River to escape New Jersey
examples: This architect also designed a house with a massive window resembling a sailboat and a large number nine next to its front door.
This architect of the Haute-Garonne Capitol Building in Toulouse included a semicircle 'sailboat' window on his Lieb House, which had to be floated up the East River to escape New Jersey.

clues.clue: Franklin Court in Philadelphia: two steel-tube 'ghost structures' in the shape of homes
examples: One building designed by this architect includes two 'ghost structures' in the shape of homes that were created from steel tubes.

clues.clue: Denise Scott Brown's essay 'Room at the Top? Sexism and the Star System in Architecture' criticized the architecture world for giving Venturi credit for coining 'duck building'
examples: This architect's partner wrote the essay 'Room at the Top? Sexism and the Star System in Architecture,' which describes how this architect was given credit for coining the term 'duck building.'

comprehensive_summary:
Robert Venturi (1925–2018) was the foremost American Postmodern architect, best known for the quip 'Less is a bore,' a pointed rebuke of Mies van der Rohe's Modernist dictum 'Less is more.' He won the Pritzker Prize in 1991, though his long-time partner and wife Denise Scott Brown refused to attend the ceremony because the prize was awarded to him alone for projects on which they had collaborated, including the Sainsbury Wing of London's National Gallery.

His most celebrated building is the Vanna House (Vanna Venturi House) in Philadelphia, designed for his mother Vanna. It features an exposed chimney, a wrap-around staircase, a gently sloping roof, and a monumental split-gable facade. Critic Frederick Schwartz called it 'the first Postmodern anything.'

Venturi co-authored two foundational texts of Postmodern architecture. Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture (1966) called for an eclectic 'messy vitality' against Modernist purity. Learning from Las Vegas (1972), written with Denise Scott Brown and Steven Izenour, celebrated Las Vegas roadside commercial architecture and introduced the distinction between 'ducks' (buildings whose form represents their function) and 'decorated sheds.' Aron Vinegar's I am a Monument is a critical examination of that book.

Other notable projects include Fire Station No. 4 in Columbus, Indiana (with a giant gold '4' making the structure look like an overturned number four); the Lieb House (a 'sailboat' window, the number nine beside the door; floated up the East River to escape New Jersey); Franklin Court in Philadelphia (two steel-tube 'ghost structures' in the shape of homes); the Seattle Art Museum; additions to the Allen Art Museum at Oberlin and to Cass Gilbert's building there; the Brant-Johnson Ski House in Vail; and the Haute-Garonne Capitol Building in Toulouse.
